Name: Sepsis patient escape room
Embed Code: <iframe width="960" height="540" data-original-width="1920" data-original-height="1080" src="https://www.thinglink.com/view/scene/1471771382449700868" type="text/html" style="border: none;" webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe><script async src="//cdn.thinglink.me/jse/responsive.js"></script>
Narrative: Students had to move through the tags in the room to view, assess and interpret the medical data. This included all the data that would be provided in a real-life sepsis situation, such as hospital admission notes, blood and urine results, heart rate and temperature and records of drugs administered. Some were provided in written or image form, another as a phone message recorded as an audio file in a tag. Each learning outcome on the sepsis pathway was included in the challenge that the doctors created. Presented on teams, students worked in groups. 45 minute timer. 360 image of real-life escape room used for training.

Name: Infographic/map about Beethoven
Embed Code: <iframe width="960" height="540" data-original-width="1920" data-original-height="1080" src="https://www.thinglink.com/view/scene/1454629934541570049" type="text/html" style="border: none;" webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe><script async src="//cdn.thinglink.me/jse/responsive.js"></script>
Narrative: Shows how powerful it can be when used alongside a consistent design on 2D images. Each scene in the tour is in fact a slightly altered version of the original background image. They are all created using the same map with different graphics laid on top. As you move between the scenes, the geographical locations mentioned in the text box are also highlighted. Move back and forth between the scenes a few times and you will soon see how this clever effect was created. The tags were then simply placed on top in the appropriate places. To the user, the transition appears seamless and we are unaware that we are in fact moving between scenes!

Name: Virtual Lab 1, Keele University
Embed Code: <iframe width="960" height="540" data-original-width="1920" data-original-height="1080" src="https://www.thinglink.com/view/scene/1368531711838650369" type="text/html" style="border: none;" webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe><script async src="//cdn.thinglink.me/jse/responsive.js"></script>
Narrative: Designed as complementary higher education tool for undergraduate, postgraduate (taught), and research students of physical sciences. The ThingLink content for the instrument simulation provided a number of different areas to explore, including background theory, technical information on how the instruments operate, health and safety information (where appropriate), sample preparation, and calibration procedures. Allowed students to analyze virtually a range of samples appropriate to their course. The instruments would undertake an “analysis” and then provide data extracted from a real life data set in a real laboratory. The materials were sent to teachers and learners, who provided feedback via questionnaires and interviews. The results showed extremely positive feedback. Results were submitted as academic research paper on use of ThingLink as teaching resource - very positive. See case study for more detail.
Creator Contacts and Socials: Dr Adam Jeffery, Keele University a.j.jeffery@keele.ac.uk

Project Type: Interactive Infographic,Interactive product demo or display
Narrative: The final ThingLink content created for the trade show drew a lot of positive feedback from visitors and other exhibitors - immediately grabbing their audience’s attention and helping Carmanah stand out from other exhibitors. The team were able to immerse the customers in the environment in which the products normally sit - showing off their full capability. The content acted as a self-selection tool – with users navigating themselves to the specific area they were interested in. To explore a different product, they clicked on the home button to return to the landing screen where they could choose one of the 4 “families” of products.

Name: Elderly Care (RAI - Resident Assessment Instrument)
Embed Code: <iframe width="960" height="540" data-original-width="1920" data-original-height="1080" src="https://www.thinglink.com/view/scenario/2009597950220567014" type="text/html" style="border: none;" webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe><script async src="//cdn.thinglink.me/jse/responsive.js"></script>
Narrative: Welcome to "Spot the Signs: Interactive RAI Training for Caregivers."This interactive learning experience is designed to help you strengthen your skills in early identification of health, cognitive, and functional changes in elderly clients, using the Resident Assessment Instrument (RAI) as your guide.As a caregiver, nurse, or support worker, your daily observations play a critical role in recognizing subtle signs that may indicate a shift in a person's health or well-being. Timely recognition and proper documentation through the RAI process can lead to earlier interventions, better care outcomes, and enhanced quality of life for the individuals you support.In this training, you'll begin with a brief overview of the RAI framework. Then, you’ll step into the home environments of two elderly individuals — Margaret and George — where you’ll be asked to observe, identify early warning signs, and connect your findings to the correct RAI domains. Finally, you’ll practice using those insights to inform care planning decisions.

Name: Elderly Care - Rai discussion SBL experience:
Embed Code: <iframe width="960" height="540" data-original-width="1920" data-original-height="1080" src="https://www.thinglink.com/view/scenario/2009609354394730982" type="text/html" style="border: none;" webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe><script async src="//cdn.thinglink.me/jse/responsive.js"></script>
Narrative: Learning ObjectiveAs a caregiver or assessor, having meaningful conversations with clients is a vital part of recognizing changes in their health and wellbeing. In this scenario, you’ll meet George, a 78-year-old man who lives at home with his wife and manages several health concerns, including COPD and occasional memory lapses.Your task is to engage in a short check-in with George and ask appropriate, RAI-informed questions to learn more about his daily life, routines, and any recent changes. Your choices will influence how much George shares with you — and how well you’re able to identify key issues that should be documented in the Resident Assessment Instrument (RAI).Pay close attention to what George says. Choose your questions thoughtfully. Some will encourage George to open up, while others may miss the mark or lead the conversation off-track.(In house example)

Narrative: These safety rules provide a clear framework to help everyone work safely, no matter their role or location. They apply to all activities whether routine or high-risk and are meant to reduce the chance of harm to people, the environment, or equipment. The rules focus on key areas where serious incidents are most likely to occur, offering practical steps to identify hazards, manage risks, and make safe decisions.By following these rules, individuals take responsibility not only for their own safety but also for the safety of those around them. The goal is to build a consistent safety culture where risks are actively managed, unsafe actions are stopped, and everyone understands what is expected every task, every time.Following these rules is not optional, it is essential to ensure a safe and respectful workplace for all.

Narrative: About the TrainingThis training is divided into three parts:1. ScenarioYou're assigned to inspect a junction box on a pylon tower in snow and icy conditions, approximately 15–20 meters above ground.Throughout the scenario you have to make choices, with each one affecting your safety and compliance. Your goal is to complete the task while maintaining continuous fall protection. Each situation is scored as follows:Compliant: 10 pointsPartially compliant: 2-7 pointsMaximum score: 40 points2. Reflection on the ScenarioThrough a guided conversation, you will reflect on the decisions made during the scenario and how winter conditions influence your safety at height.3. Practice with autumn and strong wind conditionsThis autumn scenario places you in the same role and maintenance task on a pylon tower but under wet and windy conditions.
